CORNISH: But the power dynamic is built into the whole thing.

HAYS: A thousand percent.

WASSERMAN: Yes. And when you go out to these bars, it's like a member of Congress is like hanging out a bar. And everyone wants a piece of him. It's like reporters are talking to him.

WASSERMAN: Staffers are talking to him. So, of course, it creates this weird power dynamic.

CORNISH: All right, Jacob, thank you. I hope we can have you back.

CORNISH: You guys are doing a good job kind of explaining the weird ways that we all take for granted.

CORNISH: We've sort of ignored some of this stuff. And I love that TMZ is coming in and taking a look at it. Thank you.

WASSERMAN: Thank you.

CORNISH: All right, next, we want to turn to this. Pretty serious. The president striking up a new nuclear deal. And it's not with Iran. We're going to talk about a deal with Saudi Arabia.

Plus, AOC says she's focused on the midterms. So, why is everyone else talking about her in 2028?

CHRIS WRIGHT, ENERGY SECRETARY: President Trump's agenda in the Middle East has been commerce, not conflict. Saudi Arabia has been an ally of the United States since the nation's forming. And I'm thrilled in our efforts and where this is going.

CORNISH: Commerce, not conflict. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright is hailing a deal between the U.S. and Saudi Arabia, which will grant the kingdom a civilian nuclear program. President Trump signed off Wednesday. It now goes to Congress. The deal is for 30 years. It involves American companies giving technology and expertise to the Saudi program. It allows for enriching uranium, but only to create energy, not a nuclear bomb. Some critics are not convinced.

REP. JOHN GARAMENDI (D-CA): We've already got around with a -- an ability to enrich uranium to the point of being able to make a bomb. And we're going to turn that over to Saudi Arabia? No, we're not. This is just terrible, terrible policy.

CORNISH: Joining me now, Admiral James Stavridis, CNN's senior military analyst and former NATO supreme allied commander.

I think for those of us, sort of, who were raised post 9/11, this feels really surprising and maybe almost out of left field. But I realize there may be security reasons this is happening. Can you talk about how this has been talked about prior to this point? Like, how did we get here?

ADM. JAMES STAVRIDIS (RET.), CNN SENIOR MILITARY ANALYST: It's been a conversation ongoing for well over a decade, the idea of not a nuclear weapon, and we need to be clear here, we're talking about nuclear power. Civilian use of nuclear power. But, yes, concerning any time there's proliferation and a nation gets that ability to enrich. And, of course, that's what we don't want Iran to have. So, why Saudi Arabia?

Short answer. We want to stay very aligned with the Saudis. Point two, Saudi Arabia already has a very close relationship with Pakistan and a reasonable relationship with Russia. So, I think part of the conversation here, and I agree with this, is better we should work with the Saudis. They're going to get this capability. And I'll close with this. If you're the crown prince of Saudi Arabia, who you're showing here --

STAVRIDIS: Mohammed bin Salman, and you look at your neighborhood and Israel has nuclear power and nuclear weapons. Iran has nuclear power and is striving for a nuclear weapon. I think the minimum for you, as a responsible leader of the kingdom, is to get to nuclear power and do it in concert with the United States.

CORNISH: And as you -- just because you mentioned the name of MBS, of course people -- plenty of people have talked about the human rights history in Saudi Arabia.

They are the global leader in executions. A lot of cracking down on online speech. And, of course, best known in the U.S., 2018, sanctioned the murder of Jamal Khashoggi.

And I think I want to help better understand this relationship. How is Trump different in his approach with Saudi Arabia than Biden?

STAVRIDIS: The Trump administration is willing to move further along in close alignment with the kingdom. And let's back up. Three years ago, Audie, when the horrific events of October of '23 occurred, what happened right before that? Right before that, the Biden administration was trying to bring the kingdom of Saudi Arabia and Israel together in formal recognition of each other. This is an extension of Trump one, the so-called Abraham Accords.

So, there's been a bipartisan consensus through both Biden and Trump to try and bring the kingdom closer to Israel and to keep it aligned with the United States. This is a logical extension of both of those.

CORNISH: So, I want to play one last thing for you then. Naftali Bennett, former Israeli prime minister. Here is his reaction to this.

NAFTALI BENNETT, FORMER ISRAELI PRIME MINISTER (through translator): Saudi Arabia gets nuclear capabilities without progress towards normalization, without anything in return. In all of these, Israel is not at the table, but on the table.

CORNISH: Just want to add that one more point. As you said, on the one hand, you want to get to a path of normalization. On the other hand, not sounding happy.

STAVRIDIS: Yes. Completely agree with Naftali Bennett. I know all of the senior Israeli leaders. They are all going to want to see concrete progress toward that alignment you and I just talked about before the kingdom gets to the enrichment. So, let's look at the details of the deal. Let's look at the timeline. Let's listen to the Israelis. I think if we do all those things, we can probably achieve the overarching objectives here.

CORNISH: OK. That's Admiral James Stavridis. Thank you so much for that context. Really appreciate it

CORNISH: I want to turn to this. A new University of New Hampshire poll -- that's right, we're already on New Hampshire, because the leading Democrat for president, according to them, is Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in 2028. The New York congresswoman is favored by 22 percent of likely Democratic primary voters. Pete Buttigieg is one point behind. Rounding out the top five, Mark Kelly, Gavin Newsom and Bernie Sanders.

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): I feel very, you know, humbled by the support of everyday people. I think that, you know, polls go up, polls go down. That's kind of part of how all of this works.

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: Why do you think yours are going up right now?

OCASIO-CORTEZ: You'd have to ask folks in New Hampshire.

CORNISH: OK, the group chat is back.

There's been -- whoa, this side of the, head shaking. Wait, let me start with the Democrat.

HAYS: I mean, I just -- none of those five people, I think, are going to be the nominee for the Democratic Party come 2028. I think we're a long way from that. But I do think that people see AOC as a fighter. And I think they see her pushing back on Trump and also pushing back on members of her own party. And I think that's important to Democrats right now.

I also think that it's really interesting to watch how she -- who she is endorsing in the midterms versus someone like a Bernie Sanders.

HAYS: She is much more conservative and much more thorough with her endorsements, which I think shows her willingness to be more selective and also her probably desire for higher office. I'm not sure she will run for president. I see her more running for Senate.

CORNISH: But what if she sounded like this when she talked to the voters. A name and image you might recognize. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

BARACK OBAMA, FORMER U.S. PRESIDENT (November 4, 2008): And bend it once more toward the hope of a better day. It's been a long time coming, but tonight, because of what we did on this day, in this election, at this defining moment, change has come to America.

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY) (May 28, 2026): Ask your skeptical neighbor to believe. It's the only thing that we have. Our hope is the only thing that creates change.

CORNISH: Trying out a little something?

THOMPSON: I mean, I was following AOC around this last weekend throughout Michigan, and the first -- the first speech, I was like, huh, that sounds -- that sounds a bit familiar. And then I just went back. I literally went back through all these Obama speeches from the 2007 campaign. And there is similar rhetoric.

And what's interesting about her doing this is it is an attempt to expand her appeal beyond just, you know, Democratic socialist revolutionaries.

She's also positioning herself as a change -- potential change candidate. And as -- you know, people sometimes forget that Obama really was an insurgency against the party establishment of his time. And she very much is trying to -- it's not just about Democratic socialism, it is about fighting off, you know, generational change and also just changing the status of the party.

CORNISH: Yes, I'm surprised. I mean, honestly, I feel like it's been a hot minute and I expected to hear more of this rhetoric from more candidates. In the meantime, the media is doing what you're doing. We're looking at all the names we can possibly look at. OK, Mark Kelly is being reported in "The Atlantic Council." "The New York Magazine" going all in with, like it or not, Kamala Harris is the front runner. And then Pete Buttigieg emerging. That is the word from "The Advocate." So, everyone's taking (INAUDIBLE).

DUBKE: We should -- we should bring Jacob back because I feel like that's a TMZ list.

DUBKE: Of Democrats who have been on camera the most. Gavin Newsom at six percent. He's going to unbutton one more button of his shirt.

CORNISH: That's a different job.

DUBKE: So, that's what we're going to get.

CORNISH: Yes. DUBKE: But she's only one point ahead of Mayor Pete, who hasn't really been campaigning. So, I don't --

CORNISH: That's not true. Mayor Pete is everywhere.

HAYS: Mayor Pete did just invest in all 50 states and giving money. So, he is doing things to actively strengthen the Democratic Party. But you're -- you know, to your point --

HAYS: This is all about name recognition right now.

HAYS: And who's out there right now. And AOC is out there.

CORNISH: Whose name can you remember?

HAYS: And she's fighting, right?

DUBKE: And that's exactly what Democrats need.

DUBKE: And Mark Kelly -- and Mark Kelly has risen to the top because, again, of Donald Trump going after him. So --

THOMPSON: There's an extra level of intrigue going on this week because the DNC is meeting to decide which states go first in the 2028 primary calendar.

CORNISH: Oh, this thing.

THOMPSON: And AOC and Bernie would have an advantage -- or a left wing candidate would have an advantage in New Hampshire or Nevada.

CORNISH: In New Hampshire, yes.

THOMPSON: Because Bernie won Nevada by almost 50 percent last time. If South Carolina goes first --

CORNISH: Instead of a South Carolina.

THOMPSON: And so there's a lot of behind the scenes jockeying going on this week.

HAYS: I recommend a five, four, two, one strategy. It wins the presidency. But that's just my personal opinion here.

CORNISH: We're going to -- because 2028 is a ways away, we will come back to this. (INAUDIBLE) this.

DUBKE: But as Republicans, we don't change. We keep it the same way year after year.

CORNISH: Yes. You do. You do. You're hardcore.

All right, because it's Thursday and people online get mad at me if we don't do the group chats, we're doing the group chat.

We're going to start with Meghan.

HAYS: Sure. So, there's a sleeper hit summer series, "The Five-Star Weekend" on Peacock, which I ran into last weekend to binge. Jennifer Garner is in it.

CORNISH: Is this Chloe Sevigny and a couple other people?

HAYS: It's after a book. It's very good. It's not as light-hearted as you would think it was when I first turned it on, but it's -- I recommend it to everyone.

CORNISH: Nice. What about you?

DUBKE: Oh, mine's preachy. It's, be bad at something.

CORNISH: I like this.

DUBKE: It's really -- it's so -- it's this time, and I was talking --

HAYS: That's why we show up every Thursday.

I was talking to my wife about this.

DUBKE: Try something new. Like --

CORNISH: And don't be afraid to fail.

DUBKE: And don't be afraid to fail because when we get older we stop doing new things. We stop beginning new things. We do and repeat and get in a rut about all the stuff we're good at. And so, try something new. Be bad.

CORNISH: I love this. We're going to talk after because I have been trying to do things lately, and it's taken me to some very strange place.

DUBKE: Probably in a good way. CORNISH: Very funny. In a good way. In a good way.

DUBKE: In a good way.

CORNISH: Now, you and I saw "The Odyssey."

CORNISH: So, what's in my group chat is Elon Musk's follow-up to his whinging (ph) about the casting by saying he is going to deliver -- someone was like, I want you to give Mel Gibson $100 million to film your own. And then there are people who have been putting out their own versions of "The Odyssey" with the cast they imagine. And I just -- I love that for them. It's like, if you want to swallow slop, here's a spoon. I would much rather choose Nolan.

THOMPSON: Well, this is -- this is part of my larger group chat, which is Greek mythology is back, baby. And everyone --

CORNISH: And all -- and all the men who are into the Roman empire.

CORNISH: This is your time. Rise up.

THOMPSON: But even, you know, and every --

DUBKE: That's a pick-up line on Capitol Hill, by the way.

HAYS: That Congress is using.

DUBKE: Let me -- let me tell you about my Greek history.

CORNISH: My God, where is the sea monster when you need them?

THOMPSON: I also -- I also love Elon Musk being like, I want the historically accurate version of "The Odyssey, when it's not history. Like, it's --

THOMPSON: But -- but, you know, everyone -- I think it's -- it's great, in my opinion. Like, me and my nerdy friends, like, I just started the -- Stephen Fry's version of "Troy" last night.

THOMPSON: Like, we're talking about Greek mythology in a way that I haven't since high school.

CORNISH: We are. People are obsessed.

CORNISH: The amount -- I know this is --

HAYS: This is not what we're doing over here. We are not doing this.

THOMPSON: But we're debating the Edith Wilson version versus the previous version.

HAYS: We're watching reality TV, going to sporting events live.

CORNISH: You might like -- words and (ph) translation.

THOMPSON: I bought the thirsty (ph) book. Like things --

DUBKE: There are 10 -- there are at least ten versions of "The Odyssey" that are out there. 2001, "A Space Odyssey."

CORNISH: There's been --

THOMPSON: Well, 11 now with Elon.

DUBKE: Well, yes, 11.

"SpongeBob SquarePants" movie, that is based on "The Odessey."

CORNISH: Also "Odyssey."

CORNISH: It's all a homeric (ph) journey.

CORNISH: I appreciate that.

DUBKE: Part of your friend group, are you watching "SpongeBob"? Have you seen "O Brother Where Art Thou"?

THOMPSON: Not yet. I was not aware of the "SpongeBob."

CORNISH: Wait, you don't know this?

THOMPSON: Maybe it's because I don't have kids. I don't know. I didn't watch the "SpongeBob" movie.

CORNISH: Listen, I mean it's also -- it's a testament to the idea of a hero's journey and this story that is so old is embedded in all of our storytelling to this day.

Like, and I think that's really fascinating.

DUBKE: And who hasn't told their wife, I know I should have been home after ten minutes of a short journey, but it took me 10 years --

THOMPSON: But ran to Charlize Theron and --

DUBKE: It took me ten years to it (ph).

CORNISH: If it makes you feel --

HAYS: Remember (ph) the Congress again telling their wives --

CORNISH: Meghan, Penelope had many suitors, so it could have been the bachelorette of our time.

All right, I'm Audie Cornish. The serious news is coming up, I promise you. "NEWS CENTRAL" is next.

Return to Transcripts main page

Polls Show Iran War Unpopular as Gas Prices Rise; Nolan Wells's Cause of Death 'Undetermined'. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 23, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: Excellent work there, Spidey. And just in time for the new film, as well.

I mean, maybe this is promotion that we just don't know about. Who knows? But a very fitting color for his JEEP, as well, there. Spidey, good work.

That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS, everyone. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: OK. Today in the group chat, the president telling Americans are not against war with Iran. Polls are telling us a different story. Is the GOP prepared to own this war heading into the midterms?

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: Will there be backlash in November?

SEN. RAND PAUL (R-KY): Probably.

CORNISH: And the U.S. just signed a nuclear deal with the kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Could that lead to a dangerous arms race in the Middle East?

CHRISTINE WONSLEY, MOTHER OF NOLAN WELLS: The only question that we have is what happened to our son. That -- that is the only question.

CORNISH: An independent autopsy raises more questions about the death of young Nolan Wells. When can his family get the answers they seek?

And Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez leads a new primary poll of Democrats for president. Is she trying to channel Obama and be the hope and change candidate?

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): Our hope is the only thing that that creates change.

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: Americans aren't against the war. The -- a poll -- a poll just came out. Americans don't want high gasoline prices, but they're not against the war. That just came out loud and clear in a poll.

CORNISH: President Trump says his war with Iran is popular. We're going to look at the polls actually say, as the U.S. military carries out a 12th straight night of airstrikes inside Iran.

Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and I'm going to show you poll after poll finding a war that is historically unpopular, and in record time.

New "Washington Post" survey finds 68 percent saying the war is not worth fighting, 40 points higher than those who think it is. The Iraq and Afghanistan wars never reached that mark.

And just two months into the war, 61 percent believe the war was a mistake. It took Americans nearly four years to reach the same conclusion about the war in Iraq.

RAJU: So, are you, as Republicans prepared to own this heading into November, given its unpopularity, the war?

REP. MIKE JOHNSON (R-LA): What we're voting on right now is $60 billion to make sure that our troops are supplied. We have the munitions we need to defend America. It is shameful to me that Democrats are unwilling to go along on that.

CORNISH: Joining me now in the group chat, Alex Thompson CNN political analyst and national political reporter at Axios; Mike Dubke, former communications director at the Trump White House; and Meghan Hays, former director of message and planning at the Biden White House. Welcome back, everybody.

Alex, I want to start with you, because yesterday on this panel, we talked a lot about the bill itself: Hegseth asking for this money, what they want it for, and whether we're actually even getting the accurate number of how much they will need going forward.

Today, I want to talk to you about the politics of this. What do you know, either through Congress or coverage of the White House, about how the White House is approaching this? Because the president is just sort of like, things are going great. You don't know what you're talking about.

ALEX THOMPSON, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST: I mean, the White House is basically saying, are you going to vote against the troops or are you going to vote against the munitions?

The fact is that this war has brought a really dangerous shortage of a lot of our munitions. Like, the American military, is not -- if we are attacked or if there's anything else, we're in trouble.

And so, the White House, basically, has been, like, we need this money because of the crisis and this munitions shortage that we created.

CORNISH: What do you think is the disconnect, then, between the voter and the White House?

MIKE DUBKE, FORMER TRUMP WHITE HOUSE COMMUNICATIONS DIRECTOR: The voter and the White House?

CORNISH: Yes. Meaning why would this poll show people think it's literally not worth fighting?

DUBKE: Because I think that -- because I think that's a different question than you were just asking. The question that the voters are facing in that is, "Was it worth it?" And I think that was the exact quote, actually, from the poll. Was the incursion and the conflict with -- with Iran worth it?

And I think they're looking at this going, We haven't been sold on it being worth it.

That is a very, very different question than whether or not we need to fund our troops that are currently under active fire, and whether or not we need to re- -- re-supply our munitions.

DUBKE: And that's a very different question that's happening on Capitol Hill.

CORNISH: Here's a taste of what they're showing on FOX News. Just to give an example.

BRIT HUME, FOX NEWS ANCHOR: He promised to keep us out of -- of endless wars or unsatisfactory wars. And now he's in one. And it's not at all clear how it's going to end.

LAURA INGRAHAM, FOX NEWS HOST: With the midterm elections a little more than 100 days away, the clock is ticking for President Trump to get out of this war before voters head to the polls.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: It's interesting to me that people are talking about this in this way. They are not saying this is time to -- we've got to support the troop. We already are in there. There's nothing we can do. We broke it, we bought it. It's not that argument.

It's maybe this was a bad call, but we're here now. And I think the question is, will voters still punish people for the bad call?

MEGHAN HAYS, FORMER BIDEN WHITE HOUSE DIRECTOR OF MESSAGE PLANNING: Yes, absolutely. And it's a bad call, because our gas prices are too high, and they can't afford meat and coffee at the grocery store. And so, that is what they're grappling with.

But that is a very different question of should we be supporting our troops? And should we be funding our ammunition restocking? And that is something that Democrats are going to have to grapple with, because do you give the Pentagon more money to go to a war that no one wants to be in that's costing the American people money, that's costing heartache at home?

Or are you saying, no, we're not going to give you money? And then, that puts our troops in danger.

CORNISH: With more service members killed in action recently.

THOMPSON: Well, this is one of the Democrats' only pieces of leverage, because the Republicans have done nothing to force a vote on whether or not to even authorize this military action.

So, the Democrats are using this opportunity for this moment of leverage.

CORNISH: I also want to show you gas prices. I think during the Biden administration, there was a little bit of, like, don't look at this number. Look at that number. Don't worry about this. Let's talk about democracy. And in the meantime --

DUBKE: I've got a better economist for you.

CORNISH: You've got a better. And so, here are gas prices today: 4.09. In January of 2025 before we went to war, so $3.12. And then under Biden at its peak was 5.02.

And today, as we said, when it comes to the Trump administration, we're at 4.09. And the Strait of Hormuz is not reopening.

And now you've got Houthis in Yemen deciding they're going to jump into the Red Sea. The answer to this from Trump is saying, Look, don't worry about it. We don't need it. We've got Venezuela.

I'm going to let you hear this explanation.

TRUMP: You combine us and Venezuela, we have about 62 percent of the world's oil market. So, we don't need straits. We don't need anything. We don't need the Hormuz Street [SIC]. But we do it because we have to do it, because we cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon.

CORNISH: So, the U.S. is only in this position, because it became an exporter of energy at a certain point, right? We're doing pretty well.

Venezuela, I was under the impression, had an enormous earthquake recently. And oil companies are wondering how they're even going to do the work that they're promising there.

THOMPSON: Also, any oil executive will tell you cannot -- it's not -- it's not like a faucet. You can't just suddenly have tons of oil flowing. It takes years of building up infrastructure. A lot of that infrastructure had been degraded in Venezuela.

CORNISH: And we're eating into our reserves.

THOMPSON: Absolutely.

HAYS: And didn't they go to the White House and say it's not economically feasible to go to Venezuela and build that infrastructure back up? So, I mean --

THOMPSON: Yes, but there have been some deals the Trump administration has --

THOMPSON: -- has signed. But it's just the very -- it's just the very beginning.

And also, as you noted, we're a net exporter, but it's a global market. The Strait of Hormuz affects the price of oil everywhere, which is why, even though we are a net exporter, the prices are still up.

DUBKE: We didn't get oil from the Strait of Hormuz. That's the other part of this.

CORNISH: But when the president announced a ceasefire, the thing he said is if we didn't stop, there might be a global recession. Like, he said that when he explained to everyone why he wanted a pause in fighting.

So, it's hard not to picture a return to fighting leading to the scenario he introduced, which is a global recession.

DUBKE: Well, they -- so the memorandum of understanding created a situation in which there was some oil that moved out. But again, we are getting perilously, perilously low on world reserves. And so, yes, are we coming back to that position? I think so. We've got a little bit of time on that. The bigger question, though, here is on the American exporting. If we

just kept our oil and our -- and our gas, we'd be fine. But we're not. We are a net export. We are an exporter now.

CORNISH: They're trying to keep prices down.

DUBKE: And -- and in order to do that, in order to keep prices low in Europe and other places in the world, we're going to potentially see some shortages.

CORNISH: Well, I'm sure we're going to talk more about this. I'm fascinated, because I think from the Biden era, we learned that the White House can say all it wants about how well things are going, but if people don't think it's -- feel it's going well, nothing can save you.

So, coming up on CNN THIS MORNING, there is a different kind of fight going on, on Capitol Hill. This one is over billions of dollars for the military. And we're going to talk about how that is working out this morning.

Plus, the fight to find out how young Nolan Wells died is far from over. The new autopsy that raises more questions than answers.

WONSLEY: The only question that we have is what happened to our son. That -- that is the only question.

CORNISH: All right. But there are more questions than answers for the family of Nolan Wells after an independent autopsy could not determine what caused the 18-year-old's death.

Wells's body was found off the coast of an island in Mississippi, where he had gone boating with friends for the Fourth of July. He never returned home.

The private autopsy found no fractures or deep tissue injuries, but it did note a 12-by-8-inch area of red discoloration on the back of Wells's head.

Now, the family attorney says the findings were limited, because Wells's body was not intact from the initial autopsy ordered by authorities.

BENJAMIN CRUMP, ATTORNEY FOR NOLAN WELLS'S FAMILY: For these reasons, this pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening, non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells. Understand? (END VIDEO CLIP)

CORNISH: All right. Joining me now to discuss, former prosecutor Alyse Adamson.

Alyse, thank you so much for coming on.

A lot of people are talking about this online. And first, I want to talk about this independent autopsy, which means it's happening after the official one.

But that also means they did not receive what's called an intact body. There were aspects of his organs that were not available to this independent person doing the autopsy.

How common or uncommon is that? And if it is common, what can you really get out of such limited findings?

ALYSE ADAMSON, FORMER PROSECUTOR: Yes. Thank you, Audie.

You know, I would say it's relatively common. Autopsies are routine in cases of unexplained death, and medical examiners need to follow a certain protocol to get to the answers.

Here, what was missing, significantly missing from the independent autopsy was the neck structure. That is because the neck can tell us a lot. Was the hyoid bone intact, for instance? That would be an indicia of strangulation, if it was not.

It can also tell you a lot about a potential drowning. Those are some of the critical questions in this case. And so, that's why the state medical examiners had removed it for examination.

So, in a case where that -- this isn't the central question. Perhaps the body could have given more information. But here, where the neck is so significant, it is -- it is not. It is not uncommon or surprising that the medical examiner was not able to reach a conclusion.

And that is why the findings of the state examination are going to be so significant in the case, because they did examine that throat structure.

CORNISH: You know, I want to play for you Nancy Grace, who's the noted sort of true crime, you know, investigator. She's jumped on this. And I think it just sort of reflects how much interest there is. And all of these sort of armchair investigators that are now looking at this case. We'll talk on the other side.

NANCY GRACE, HOST, FOX'S "CRIME STORIES": So, am I left to believe that he goes out swimming all on his own? And the friend girl leaves; the mates from school leave. Everybody just leaves him out in the water, and he dies? And then just conveniently washes ashore? Somebody just left him out in the water. Does that make sense to you? (END VIDEO CLIP)

CORNISH: Is this going to add more pressure to investigators to give more answers to both the family and the public?

ADAMSON: Yes, I think so, Audie. I mean, first of all, you have the family's lawyer, Ben Crump, who's been out in front from day one. So, that alone, and the releasing of this independent autopsy has put a lot of pressure on investigators.

But you're right. Now, the national media has picked this up. And Nancy Grace is expressing what a lot of people are thinking, which is there's a lot of gaps. There are a lot of questions. And right now, because there's so many evidentiary gaps, it does look a little bit suspicious. The story doesn't make sense, because we don't have the full story.

So, you can bet that investigators are working around the clock to try to piece that together. And the autopsy, let's remember, is just one puzzle piece for the larger picture. We also need the witness statements, GPS, digital evidence.

ADAMSON: There's a lot of information that's not been public.

CORNISH: One more thing for you related to that.

CORNISH: You mentioned witnesses. One of the -- one of his -- the people who were present for that trip -- his name is Warren Hudson -- was speaking to a kind of manosphere, MAGA-ish podcast. It's called "The Officer Tatum Show."

And I found it interesting that he was sort of coming out publicly to a very specific outlet, which has broken up this interview into multiple parts. So, we don't have a lot of detail here.

But I want to give you an example of the kind of statement he's making publicly. It asks whether this kind of thing can come into play, if there's a case later. Here it is.

WARREN HUDSON, FRIEND OF NOLAN WELLS: Everybody's drinking, you know. I mean, it's Fourth of July. Everyone's just trying to enjoy themselves. And, you know, it's a big party.

It was just a big -- I talked to Nolan and, you know, Nolan was drinking. Everybody was drinking. Most of everybody. And I mean, Nolan seemed completely fine every time I talked to him. We had just regular conversations.

CORNISH: Everyone who speaks now, is this something that we might be kind of combing over later?

ADAMSON: Yes. It's interesting that he gave that interview to a podcaster and not investigators. That's not to say he hasn't spoken to investigators.

But yes, I mean, if he's ultimately called to trial, those statements could potentially come in, especially if he makes inconsistent statements.

And look, we're still waiting for the toxicology report. You just heard him say Nolan seemed fine. Well, we'll see. Was he intoxicated? Was he under the influence? Was he sober? Again, we don't know. And that's why we need to wait for more information to come out.

And the more witnesses talk, the more we try to piece it together. But that is the job for investigators. And we will see if these accounts hold up with the corroborating evidence or not.

CORNISH: Before I let you go, I just want to play for you Christine Wonsley. She is the mother of Nolan Wells. And she had to come out after this conversation about this autopsy, which had to be the most heartbreaking thing I have seen in some time. Here she is.

WONSLEY: It is our hope that our local authorities will do what they need to do, because us having the preliminary autopsy results and hearing nothing from them, it's heartbreaking. And it's concerning. Which again, is why we are here, why we have asked for this help.

CORNISH: Alyse, what are you going to be listen -- listening for in the next few days?

ADAMSON: Yes, absolutely. Tragic case. Devastating to hear her -- her talk. I'll be looking for that state medical examiner report, again because they had the full body to examine. We should have more answers.

The independent was undetermined. We need to know, are they going to classify it accidental homicide, natural causes? What?

We're also going to be looking for the investigators to share more information. Again, is there going to be witness accounts that they want to tell us about? Digital evidence? That's what I'll be looking for in the immediate couple of days.

We have heard the state say that they're going to present this all to a grand jury. That's going to take a while, but the grand jury is ultimately going to decide whether or not there's evidence of a potential crime here. So, that is the short term I'm going to be looking for. And then what

I'm going to be tracking in the long term. But just an absolutely tragic story, Audie. And I hope that investigators are able to come to some conclusions relatively soon to give this family some peace.

CORNISH: OK. Alyse Adamson, thank you. Stay with us. We'll be back in a moment.

CORNISH: OK, it's now 26 minutes past the hour. Here are five things that are making the news this morning.

So, a preliminary hearing will be back underway this morning in the murder case against the singer D4vd. Prosecutors are laying out their case in hopes of a judge taking it to trial.

Yesterday in court, they focused on samples of possible blood stains that were found in the singer's garage. It's where they are, alleging that he dismembered the body of a teenager, 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

And Tropical Storm Bertha is expected to make landfall along the Northwestern Gulf Coast today and fueling a flood threat hundreds of miles away. Forecasters say windy conditions and rain will linger on the Gulf Coast for the next few days.

Moisture linked to Bertha is feeding a flood threat that's stretching from the South to the mid-Atlantic, where some places could see a month's worth of rain -- a month's -- in just a few days.

And new questions this morning about the fatal shooting of a Houston man by an ICE agent. Two passengers who were in the van told a federal court the driver parked the vehicle before the agent fired his weapon.

Now, this contradicts ICE's claim that the officer was in danger. The shooting is still under investigation.

And hundreds of former and retired journalists calling on the White House Correspondents Association to stand up to Trump and the Trump administration's recent attacks on the media.

Now, during its rescheduled dinner on Friday, President Trump does plan to attend. The first dinner, of course, was cut short in April when a gunman opened fire in the building.

And the FDA says it is investigating a new outbreak of cyclosporiasis. It says 72 cases are tied to a source that they have yet to identify.

Now, this is linked to the outbreaks that has already caused hundreds of people to get sick across the country.

Now, straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, we're going to be talking about the U.S. and Iran continuing to trade military strikes. How the Trump administration says it will respond as the fight spreads across the region.

Plus, Speaker Mike Johnson backs a ban on lawmakers dating any staff. So, could this change the power dynamic in Washington?

segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

Johnson Backs Ban on Lawmakers Dating Any Staffers; U.S. Signals Deal with Saudi Arabia; New Democratic Presidential Poll. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 23, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish. I want to thank you for joining me on CNN THIS MORNING.

It's half past the hour. And here is what's happening right now.

President Trump issued new threats against Iran as the U.S. carries out its twelfth straight night of airstrikes against the country. Trump says that the U.S. will start bombing power plants and bridges if Iran targets ships in the Strait of Hormuz. In response, Iran now saying they'd strike back with, quote, "an eye for an eye" in the region.

And the House takes a step forward in funding some of the president's plans. House Speaker Mike Johnson helped pass the blueprint for the budget plans, including funding the war with Iran, promoting the president's SAVE America Voting Act and aid for farmers. It still faces an uncertain path through the Senate.

And House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan wants former Special Counsel Jack Smith to face perjury charges. Jordan sent a letter to the DOJ, obtained by CNN. In the letter, he claims Smith made false statements when testifying before Congress last December. Smith previously prosecuted President Trump.

Now, if House Speaker Mike Johnson has his way, there's another rule in Congress that may come to pass, a no dating zone.

We're talking full stop. Johnson is supporting a ban on lawmakers getting romantically involved with House staffers. And that includes the staffers who don't work for them.

REP. MIKE JOHNSON (R-LA): It's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace. And most companies have that. We should -- we should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have a, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America. So, that's an easy one for me. (END VIDEO CLIP)

CORNISH: So, why would this be a problem? Well, Republican Congresswoman Kat Cammack of Florida says, "what we have seen is a culture of creepiness. I wouldn't feel comfortable sending my daughter to work here on Capitol Hill."

The group chat is back.

And we're bringing in Jacob Wasserman, co-managing editor at TMZ DC.

Thank you so much for being here, Jacob.

JACOB WASSERMAN, CO-MANAGING EDITOR, TMZ DC: Thank you for having me.

CORNISH: So, one of the reasons why I wanted to talk to you is because I have seen you online. You're very tapped into the culture on The Hill. Literally, how people are talking to each other, what they're like in the hallways. And the rest of us all have the sort of above board experience of just covering stuff. So, like, Mike, what are the rules, right? Like, was it that you just couldn't date your own staffers, or is it -- this would be a radical change?

MIKE DUBKE, FORMER TRUMP WHITE HOUSE COMMUNICATIONS DIRECTOR: I am a terrible person to ask, because I'm actually married to a co-worker that I met on a political campaign. So --

CORNISH: Fair. On a political campaign. That's true.

CORNISH: Many couples that are like that.

DUBKE: So, yes, no. But I think -- whatever the creepiness factor is, that should be your guide. If it feels creepy, walk away.

MEGHAN HAYS, FORMER BIDEN WHTIE HOUSE DIRECTOR OF MESSAGE PLANNING: That's right.

CORNISH: But, and yet -- and yet, and yet, and yet. Right now the number of people investigated for sexual misconduct in Congress. Eric Swalwell, 2026, right, misconduct included with a staffer. Tony Gonzalez, sexual relationship with a staffer, one that reportedly ended with her mental health problems. And then Cory Mills, currently still there, sexual misconduct and partner violence. The famous Matt Gaetz in Florida, he of Venmo (ph). And then George Santos, who we all know is somebody perhaps with campaign finance issues, he actually was dealing with a complaint about sexual harassment of a staffer.

WASSERMAN: I have a theory on this. I think a lot of the people who are elected in Congress are becoming cool for the first time in their lives. They basically come from maybe small towns across the country. Maybe you owned like a car dealership or something like that. And then, boom, you become this star. You get a social media following. And everyone has, in their own mind, what it means to be cool. Some -- for some people it's like taking photos with celebrities. We see politicians do that. Some people, it's going on cable news and having like a big opinion. And for some people, it's women.

WASSERMAN: And I think they like to use their power to take advantage of maybe staffers or people who work on The Hill. And that's why people feel uncomfortable, like that woman said about sending their daughter here.

CORNISH: I think that is a really good point. People can get high on their own supply. If you think back to someone like Anthony Weiner, what you're saying is exactly follows, someone who gets big on the internet, someone who starts to kind of believe their own press about who they are and the ego that follows.

I think the question that people are worried about, and, Alex, maybe you can talk about this, is that's veered into abuse. And that's veered into real problems politically. Like Graham Platner, whatever people are saying about those allegations, again, it's the persons relationships with women that are haunting them in the public policy space.

ALEX THOMPSON, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST: Well, and, you know, to add to the point, you know, we've all been around The Hill where, you know, some of those guys, they get their chest puffed out with their Congress pin on and, you know, they get -- they're getting, you know, flirt -- they're being flirted with by all these other -- by all these staffers. Sometimes it's, you know, it's the handsome male staffers.

CORNISH: Yes. Because we should be clear, there are consensual relationships.

CORNISH: What Mike said is not that unusual at all.

THOMPSON: Well, to -- I mean, right -- the right --

CORNISH: Plenty of people have met their spouses --

CORNISH: Probably because they never leave the office. And the question is how that veers into abuse.

THOMPSON: Absolutely.

CORNISH: Jacob, do you think part of this is generational? Meaning, like, I feel like your era, post Me Too -- I know, I'm just going to say it, Jacob, OK, your era post Me Too. You guys have a much clearer sense of gray zones, problematic behavior, willingness to call it out.

WASSERMAN: Yes. I mean, I'm sure you're absolutely right because it's now talked about in the workplace so much. Everyone goes through these training programs. They know what to look out for.

So, the interesting part, though, is there's so much overlap between politicians, members of the media and staffers when you go out to bars. There are all these bars around D.C., and everyone's hanging out with each other. And it's like loosening the tie and then it just feels like you get alcohol in the mix and, you know, the rules are off.

CORNISH: That's a good point. I mean, all beat building seems to begin over a bar, over alcohol.

THOMPSON: Yes. I mean the era of work -- Mike Johnson is right, the era of workplace romances ended in corporate America post Me Too but it has not hit Capitol Hill, in part because you have this, you know, at times boozy culture, endless happy hours. And, you know, on Congress, it seemed that they had an out.

Now, Mike Johnson, you know, comes from a pretty conservative, culturally conservative place.

CORNISH: Yes. He says he's not worried. Yes.

THOMPSON: It's going to -- it's going to be curious if there's any -- if there's any -- especially members of Congress that vocally come out against this and be like, well, I still wanted to have a little bit of a dating life.

THOMPSON: And it's been conspicuous (INAUDIBLE).

DUBKE: I seem to remember a Coldplay concert in which corporate romance didn't end in the -- in the boardroom.

DUBKE: And you're not mentioning lobbyist people.

CORNISH: We are not --

DUBKE: Come on. Let's --

CORNISH: That -- lobbyists. Well, hold on, let's let the -- a woman talk on this panel, OK. Just for --

DUBKE: I'm leaning back.

CORNISH: For the fun of it. DUBKE: Go. Go.

CORNISH: Tell me how you -- I felt like when I was covering The Hill, first of all, at the time, it was like you're wearing high heels because they were very weird about the dress code.

CORNISH: So, I'm shimmying around in this sheath dress, destroying my knee cartilage, for why? For what? You know what I mean?

CORNISH: And it was this sense that you were part of a display.

CORNISH: And all the staffers were hot. And it was confusing to me what I was a part of.

HAYS: One of my first jobs in Washington, my boss told me they prefer us to wear dresses, and the shorter the better. So that wasn't that long ago that that was the culture. And I think there were members of Congress who demanded that their staffers wore dresses. That was part of their dress code. So, I mean, that is not unusual here.

I do think that the thing that's a little bit unfortunate that we even have to have this conversation, like why can't you be the leader that you're elected to be by the people? And I think that's what's different than in corporate America.

CORNISH: But is this --

HAYS: You were elected --

CORNISH: I think this is leadership of -- I mean Johnson doesn't have to do this either (ph).

HAYS: I'm sorry, the -- right, but the fact that we even have -- that Johnson even has to do this, or is even talked about is sort of gross.

HAYS: And I think it's the inappropriate nature. I think if there is a single member of Congress who is dating someone that is age appropriate in another -- in another office, go forth and conquer.

HAYS: I hope you have a happy life together, just like you and your wife do. But that's not what's happening here. And that's not -- and these -- and those four or five cases that you put up on the screen, that's only what's been reported.

CORNISH: I was just going to say --

HAYS: Most girls and most women that are working on Congress are young and don't report them.

DUBKE: But three of those -- but three of those are already illegal.

DUBKE: Which, under the rules of the -- of Congress, you are not supposed to date a staffer in your office.

CORNISH: Right. And these ones -- yes.

DUBKE: And three of the five are all -- are just that.

HAYS: No, but, to his point, this is exactly -- they're cool for the first time. They were nerds in high school, and now all of a sudden women are paying attention to them and they say things that they don't realize are inappropriate. And it just continues to escalate. And then there are women who feel maybe they also are cool for the first time and no one's ever paid attention to them. And then this member of Congress all of a sudden is making them feel important. It's a gross culture.

CORNISH: But the power dynamic is built into the whole thing.

HAYS: A thousand percent.

WASSERMAN: Yes. And when you go out to these bars, it's like a member of Congress is like hanging out a bar. And everyone wants a piece of him. It's like reporters are talking to him.

WASSERMAN: Staffers are talking to him. So, of course, it creates this weird power dynamic.

CORNISH: All right, Jacob, thank you. I hope we can have you back.

CORNISH: You guys are doing a good job kind of explaining the weird ways that we all take for granted.

CORNISH: We've sort of ignored some of this stuff. And I love that TMZ is coming in and taking a look at it. Thank you.

WASSERMAN: Thank you.

CORNISH: All right, next, we want to turn to this. Pretty serious. The president striking up a new nuclear deal. And it's not with Iran. We're going to talk about a deal with Saudi Arabia.

Plus, AOC says she's focused on the midterms. So, why is everyone else talking about her in 2028?

CHRIS WRIGHT, ENERGY SECRETARY: President Trump's agenda in the Middle East has been commerce, not conflict. Saudi Arabia has been an ally of the United States since the nation's forming. And I'm thrilled in our efforts and where this is going.

CORNISH: Commerce, not conflict. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright is hailing a deal between the U.S. and Saudi Arabia, which will grant the kingdom a civilian nuclear program. President Trump signed off Wednesday. It now goes to Congress. The deal is for 30 years. It involves American companies giving technology and expertise to the Saudi program. It allows for enriching uranium, but only to create energy, not a nuclear bomb. Some critics are not convinced.

REP. JOHN GARAMENDI (D-CA): We've already got around with a -- an ability to enrich uranium to the point of being able to make a bomb. And we're going to turn that over to Saudi Arabia? No, we're not. This is just terrible, terrible policy.

CORNISH: Joining me now, Admiral James Stavridis, CNN's senior military analyst and former NATO supreme allied commander.

I think for those of us, sort of, who were raised post 9/11, this feels really surprising and maybe almost out of left field. But I realize there may be security reasons this is happening. Can you talk about how this has been talked about prior to this point? Like, how did we get here?

ADM. JAMES STAVRIDIS (RET.), CNN SENIOR MILITARY ANALYST: It's been a conversation ongoing for well over a decade, the idea of not a nuclear weapon, and we need to be clear here, we're talking about nuclear power. Civilian use of nuclear power. But, yes, concerning any time there's proliferation and a nation gets that ability to enrich. And, of course, that's what we don't want Iran to have. So, why Saudi Arabia?

Short answer. We want to stay very aligned with the Saudis. Point two, Saudi Arabia already has a very close relationship with Pakistan and a reasonable relationship with Russia. So, I think part of the conversation here, and I agree with this, is better we should work with the Saudis. They're going to get this capability. And I'll close with this. If you're the crown prince of Saudi Arabia, who you're showing here --

STAVRIDIS: Mohammed bin Salman, and you look at your neighborhood and Israel has nuclear power and nuclear weapons. Iran has nuclear power and is striving for a nuclear weapon. I think the minimum for you, as a responsible leader of the kingdom, is to get to nuclear power and do it in concert with the United States.

CORNISH: And as you -- just because you mentioned the name of MBS, of course people -- plenty of people have talked about the human rights history in Saudi Arabia.

They are the global leader in executions. A lot of cracking down on online speech. And, of course, best known in the U.S., 2018, sanctioned the murder of Jamal Khashoggi.

And I think I want to help better understand this relationship. How is Trump different in his approach with Saudi Arabia than Biden?

STAVRIDIS: The Trump administration is willing to move further along in close alignment with the kingdom. And let's back up. Three years ago, Audie, when the horrific events of October of '23 occurred, what happened right before that? Right before that, the Biden administration was trying to bring the kingdom of Saudi Arabia and Israel together in formal recognition of each other. This is an extension of Trump one, the so-called Abraham Accords.

So, there's been a bipartisan consensus through both Biden and Trump to try and bring the kingdom closer to Israel and to keep it aligned with the United States. This is a logical extension of both of those.

CORNISH: So, I want to play one last thing for you then. Naftali Bennett, former Israeli prime minister. Here is his reaction to this.

NAFTALI BENNETT, FORMER ISRAELI PRIME MINISTER (through translator): Saudi Arabia gets nuclear capabilities without progress towards normalization, without anything in return. In all of these, Israel is not at the table, but on the table.

CORNISH: Just want to add that one more point. As you said, on the one hand, you want to get to a path of normalization. On the other hand, not sounding happy.

STAVRIDIS: Yes. Completely agree with Naftali Bennett. I know all of the senior Israeli leaders. They are all going to want to see concrete progress toward that alignment you and I just talked about before the kingdom gets to the enrichment. So, let's look at the details of the deal. Let's look at the timeline. Let's listen to the Israelis. I think if we do all those things, we can probably achieve the overarching objectives here.

CORNISH: OK. That's Admiral James Stavridis. Thank you so much for that context. Really appreciate it

CORNISH: I want to turn to this. A new University of New Hampshire poll -- that's right, we're already on New Hampshire, because the leading Democrat for president, according to them, is Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in 2028. The New York congresswoman is favored by 22 percent of likely Democratic primary voters. Pete Buttigieg is one point behind. Rounding out the top five, Mark Kelly, Gavin Newsom and Bernie Sanders.

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): I feel very, you know, humbled by the support of everyday people. I think that, you know, polls go up, polls go down. That's kind of part of how all of this works.

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: Why do you think yours are going up right now?

OCASIO-CORTEZ: You'd have to ask folks in New Hampshire.

CORNISH: OK, the group chat is back.

There's been -- whoa, this side of the, head shaking. Wait, let me start with the Democrat.

HAYS: I mean, I just -- none of those five people, I think, are going to be the nominee for the Democratic Party come 2028. I think we're a long way from that. But I do think that people see AOC as a fighter. And I think they see her pushing back on Trump and also pushing back on members of her own party. And I think that's important to Democrats right now.

I also think that it's really interesting to watch how she -- who she is endorsing in the midterms versus someone like a Bernie Sanders.

HAYS: She is much more conservative and much more thorough with her endorsements, which I think shows her willingness to be more selective and also her probably desire for higher office. I'm not sure she will run for president. I see her more running for Senate.

CORNISH: But what if she sounded like this when she talked to the voters. A name and image you might recognize. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

BARACK OBAMA, FORMER U.S. PRESIDENT (November 4, 2008): And bend it once more toward the hope of a better day. It's been a long time coming, but tonight, because of what we did on this day, in this election, at this defining moment, change has come to America.

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY) (May 28, 2026): Ask your skeptical neighbor to believe. It's the only thing that we have. Our hope is the only thing that creates change.

CORNISH: Trying out a little something?

THOMPSON: I mean, I was following AOC around this last weekend throughout Michigan, and the first -- the first speech, I was like, huh, that sounds -- that sounds a bit familiar. And then I just went back. I literally went back through all these Obama speeches from the 2007 campaign. And there is similar rhetoric.

And what's interesting about her doing this is it is an attempt to expand her appeal beyond just, you know, Democratic socialist revolutionaries.

She's also positioning herself as a change -- potential change candidate. And as -- you know, people sometimes forget that Obama really was an insurgency against the party establishment of his time. And she very much is trying to -- it's not just about Democratic socialism, it is about fighting off, you know, generational change and also just changing the status of the party.

CORNISH: Yes, I'm surprised. I mean, honestly, I feel like it's been a hot minute and I expected to hear more of this rhetoric from more candidates. In the meantime, the media is doing what you're doing. We're looking at all the names we can possibly look at. OK, Mark Kelly is being reported in "The Atlantic Council." "The New York Magazine" going all in with, like it or not, Kamala Harris is the front runner. And then Pete Buttigieg emerging. That is the word from "The Advocate." So, everyone's taking (INAUDIBLE).

DUBKE: We should -- we should bring Jacob back because I feel like that's a TMZ list.

DUBKE: Of Democrats who have been on camera the most. Gavin Newsom at six percent. He's going to unbutton one more button of his shirt.

CORNISH: That's a different job.

DUBKE: So, that's what we're going to get.

CORNISH: Yes. DUBKE: But she's only one point ahead of Mayor Pete, who hasn't really been campaigning. So, I don't --

CORNISH: That's not true. Mayor Pete is everywhere.

HAYS: Mayor Pete did just invest in all 50 states and giving money. So, he is doing things to actively strengthen the Democratic Party. But you're -- you know, to your point --

HAYS: This is all about name recognition right now.

HAYS: And who's out there right now. And AOC is out there.

CORNISH: Whose name can you remember?

HAYS: And she's fighting, right?

DUBKE: And that's exactly what Democrats need.

DUBKE: And Mark Kelly -- and Mark Kelly has risen to the top because, again, of Donald Trump going after him. So --

THOMPSON: There's an extra level of intrigue going on this week because the DNC is meeting to decide which states go first in the 2028 primary calendar.

CORNISH: Oh, this thing.

THOMPSON: And AOC and Bernie would have an advantage -- or a left wing candidate would have an advantage in New Hampshire or Nevada.

CORNISH: In New Hampshire, yes.

THOMPSON: Because Bernie won Nevada by almost 50 percent last time. If South Carolina goes first --

CORNISH: Instead of a South Carolina.

THOMPSON: And so there's a lot of behind the scenes jockeying going on this week.

HAYS: I recommend a five, four, two, one strategy. It wins the presidency. But that's just my personal opinion here.

CORNISH: We're going to -- because 2028 is a ways away, we will come back to this. (INAUDIBLE) this.

DUBKE: But as Republicans, we don't change. We keep it the same way year after year.

CORNISH: Yes. You do. You do. You're hardcore.

All right, because it's Thursday and people online get mad at me if we don't do the group chats, we're doing the group chat.

We're going to start with Meghan.

HAYS: Sure. So, there's a sleeper hit summer series, "The Five-Star Weekend" on Peacock, which I ran into last weekend to binge. Jennifer Garner is in it.

CORNISH: Is this Chloe Sevigny and a couple other people?

HAYS: It's after a book. It's very good. It's not as light-hearted as you would think it was when I first turned it on, but it's -- I recommend it to everyone.

CORNISH: Nice. What about you?

DUBKE: Oh, mine's preachy. It's, be bad at something.

CORNISH: I like this.

DUBKE: It's really -- it's so -- it's this time, and I was talking --

HAYS: That's why we show up every Thursday.

I was talking to my wife about this.

DUBKE: Try something new. Like --

CORNISH: And don't be afraid to fail.

DUBKE: And don't be afraid to fail because when we get older we stop doing new things. We stop beginning new things. We do and repeat and get in a rut about all the stuff we're good at. And so, try something new. Be bad.

CORNISH: I love this. We're going to talk after because I have been trying to do things lately, and it's taken me to some very strange place.

DUBKE: Probably in a good way. CORNISH: Very funny. In a good way. In a good way.

DUBKE: In a good way.

CORNISH: Now, you and I saw "The Odyssey."

CORNISH: So, what's in my group chat is Elon Musk's follow-up to his whinging (ph) about the casting by saying he is going to deliver -- someone was like, I want you to give Mel Gibson $100 million to film your own. And then there are people who have been putting out their own versions of "The Odyssey" with the cast they imagine. And I just -- I love that for them. It's like, if you want to swallow slop, here's a spoon. I would much rather choose Nolan.

THOMPSON: Well, this is -- this is part of my larger group chat, which is Greek mythology is back, baby. And everyone --

CORNISH: And all -- and all the men who are into the Roman empire.

CORNISH: This is your time. Rise up.

THOMPSON: But even, you know, and every --

DUBKE: That's a pick-up line on Capitol Hill, by the way.

HAYS: That Congress is using.

DUBKE: Let me -- let me tell you about my Greek history.

CORNISH: My God, where is the sea monster when you need them?

THOMPSON: I also -- I also love Elon Musk being like, I want the historically accurate version of "The Odyssey, when it's not history. Like, it's --

THOMPSON: But -- but, you know, everyone -- I think it's -- it's great, in my opinion. Like, me and my nerdy friends, like, I just started the -- Stephen Fry's version of "Troy" last night.

THOMPSON: Like, we're talking about Greek mythology in a way that I haven't since high school.

CORNISH: We are. People are obsessed.

CORNISH: The amount -- I know this is --

HAYS: This is not what we're doing over here. We are not doing this.

THOMPSON: But we're debating the Edith Wilson version versus the previous version.

HAYS: We're watching reality TV, going to sporting events live.

CORNISH: You might like -- words and (ph) translation.

sentences.en: New "Washington Post" survey finds 68 percent saying the war is not worth fighting, 40 points higher than those who think it is.
sentences.cn: 《华盛顿邮报》最新调查发现，68%的人认为这场战争不值得打，比认为值得打的人高出40个百分点。
sentences.structure: 主谓宾+宾语从句+比较结构
sentences.analysis: 主句为New "Washington Post" survey finds，宾语从句省略that，saying为现在分词作定语修饰68 percent，40 points higher than...为比较状语。

sentences.en: It took Americans nearly four years to reach the same conclusion about the war in Iraq.
sentences.cn: 美国人花了将近四年时间才对伊拉克战争得出同样的结论。
sentences.structure: It takes sb. time to do sth.
sentences.analysis: It为形式主语，真正主语为to reach...，nearly four years为时间状语。

sentences.en: The fact is that this war has brought a really dangerous shortage of a lot of our munitions.
sentences.cn: 事实是，这场战争导致我们许多弹药出现非常危险的短缺。
sentences.structure: 主系表+表语从句
sentences.analysis: The fact is为系表结构，that引导表语从句，从句中has brought为现在完成时，shortage of...为宾语。

sentences.en: And that is a very different question than whether or not we need to fund our troops that are currently under active fire, and whether or not we need to re-supply our munitions.
sentences.cn: 这与我们是否需要为目前处于战火中的部队提供资金，以及是否需要重新供应弹药是完全不同的问题。
sentences.structure: 主系表+比较结构+并列宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为that is a very different question，than后接两个whether引导的宾语从句，第一个从句中that are...为定语从句修饰troops。

sentences.en: But that is a very different question of should we be supporting our troops? And should we be funding our ammunition restocking?
sentences.cn: 但这是一个完全不同的问题：我们是否应该支持我们的部队？我们是否应该为弹药补充提供资金？
sentences.structure: 主系表+同位语从句（疑问句形式）
sentences.analysis: 主句为that is a very different question，of后接两个疑问句作同位语，解释question的内容。

sentences.en: And that is something that Democrats are going to have to grapple with, because do you give the Pentagon more money to go to a war that no one wants to be in that's costing the American people money, that's costing heartache at home?
sentences.cn: 这是民主党人必须应对的问题，因为你是给五角大楼更多钱去打一场没人想打、耗费美国人民金钱、给国内带来心痛的战争吗？
sentences.structure: 主系表+定语从句+原因状语从句+并列定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为that is something，that Democrats...为定语从句修饰something，because后为疑问句作原因状语，其中that no one...和that's costing...为并列定语从句修饰war。

sentences.en: The private autopsy found no fractures or deep tissue injuries, but it did note a 12-by-8-inch area of red discoloration on the back of Wells's head.
sentences.cn: 私人尸检未发现骨折或深层组织损伤，但确实注意到威尔斯后脑勺有一块12乘8英寸的红色变色区域。
sentences.structure: 并列句，but连接
sentences.analysis: 第一个分句为简单主谓宾，第二个分句中did为强调，note为谓语，a 12-by-8-inch area...为宾语。

sentences.en: For these reasons, this pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening, non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells.
sentences.cn: 基于这些原因，这位病理学家无法排除任何干预性的非意外因素是否促成或导致了诺兰·威尔斯的死亡。
sentences.structure: 主谓宾+宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为this pathologist cannot rule out，if引导宾语从句，从句中contributed to or caused为并列谓语。

sentences.en: And that is why the findings of the state examination are going to be so significant in the case, because they did examine that throat structure.
sentences.cn: 这就是为什么州检查的结果在此案中如此重要，因为他们确实检查了喉咙结构。
sentences.structure: 主系表+表语从句+原因状语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为that is why...，why引导表语从句，because引导原因状语从句，did为强调。

sentences.en: And the more witnesses talk, the more we try to piece it together, but that is the job for investigators.
sentences.cn: 目击者说得越多，我们就越努力拼凑真相，但那是调查人员的工作。
sentences.structure: the more..., the more...结构+并列句
sentences.analysis: 前半部分为the more..., the more...比较结构，but连接并列句。

sentences.en: Trump says that the U.S. will start bombing power plants and bridges if Iran targets ships in the Strait of Hormuz.
sentences.cn: 特朗普表示，如果伊朗 targeting 霍尔木兹海峡的船只，美国将开始轰炸发电厂和桥梁。
sentences.structure: 主句（Trump says）+ 宾语从句（that the U.S. will start bombing... if Iran targets...）
sentences.analysis: 这是一个复合句，主句为Trump says，后接that引导的宾语从句。宾语从句中包含一个if引导的条件状语从句，表示假设情况。

sentences.en: Johnson is supporting a ban on lawmakers getting romantically involved with House staffers, and that includes the staffers who don't work for them.
sentences.cn: 约翰逊支持禁止议员与众议院工作人员建立恋爱关系，这包括那些不为他们工作的工作人员。
sentences.structure: 主句（Johnson is supporting a ban）+ 介词短语（on lawmakers getting...）+ 并列句（and that includes...）+ 定语从句（who don't work for them）
sentences.analysis: 主句为Johnson is supporting a ban，后接on引导的介词短语说明禁令内容。and连接并列句，其中that指代前文，who引导定语从句修饰staffers。

sentences.en: I think a lot of the people who are elected in Congress are becoming cool for the first time in their lives.
sentences.cn: 我认为很多当选国会议员的人一生中第一次变得很酷。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（a lot of the people... are becoming cool...）+ 定语从句（who are elected in Congress）
sentences.analysis: 主句为I think，后接省略that的宾语从句。宾语从句中主语a lot of the people由who引导的定语从句修饰。

sentences.en: And I think they like to use their power to take advantage of maybe staffers or people who work on The Hill.
sentences.cn: 我认为他们喜欢利用权力来占工作人员或国会山工作人员的便宜。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（they like to use their power...）+ 不定式短语（to take advantage of...）+ 定语从句（who work on The Hill）
sentences.analysis: 主句为I think，后接宾语从句。宾语从句中不定式短语作目的状语，who引导定语从句修饰people。

sentences.en: I think the question that people are worried about, and, Alex, maybe you can talk about this, is that's veered into abuse.
sentences.cn: 我认为人们担心的问题，亚历克斯，也许你可以谈谈这个，就是这已经变成了虐待。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（the question... is that's veered into abuse）+ 定语从句（that people are worried about）+ 插入语（and, Alex, maybe you can talk about this）
sentences.analysis: 主句为I think，后接宾语从句。宾语从句主语the question由that引导的定语从句修饰，插入语分隔了主谓。

sentences.en: And what's interesting about her doing this is it is an attempt to expand her appeal beyond just, you know, Democratic socialist revolutionaries.
sentences.cn: 她这样做有趣的一点是，这是一种尝试，将她的吸引力扩展到不仅仅是民主社会主义革命者。
sentences.structure: 主语从句（What's interesting about her doing this）+ 系动词（is）+ 表语从句（it is an attempt...）
sentences.analysis: what引导的主语从句作主语，系动词is后接表语从句。表语从句中不定式短语作定语修饰attempt。

sentences.en: And as you -- just because you mentioned the name of MBS, of course people -- plenty of people have talked about the human rights history in Saudi Arabia.
sentences.cn: 正如你——仅仅因为你提到了MBS的名字，当然人们——很多人谈论过沙特阿拉伯的人权历史。
sentences.structure: 状语从句（As you...）+ 插入语（just because you mentioned...）+ 主句（plenty of people have talked about...）
sentences.analysis: as引导方式状语从句，插入语由because引导的原因状语从句构成，主句说明事实。

sentences.en: I think if we do all those things, we can probably achieve the overarching objectives here.
sentences.cn: 我认为如果我们做了所有这些事情，我们很可能能够实现这里的总体目标。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（if we do... we can achieve...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为I think，后接宾语从句。宾语从句中包含if引导的条件状语从句，主句为we can achieve。

sentences.en: And what's interesting about her doing this is it is an attempt to expand her appeal beyond just, you know, Democratic socialist revolutionaries.
sentences.cn: 她这样做有趣的一点是，这是一种尝试，将她的吸引力扩展到不仅仅是民主社会主义革命者。
sentences.structure: 主语从句（What's interesting about her doing this）+ 系动词（is）+ 表语从句（it is an attempt...）
sentences.analysis: what引导的主语从句作主语，系动词is后接表语从句。表语从句中不定式短语作定语修饰attempt。

sentences.en: I think that's a really good point, people can get high on their own supply.
sentences.cn: 我认为这是一个非常好的观点，人们可能会自我陶醉。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（that's a really good point）+ 同位语（people can get high on their own supply）
sentences.analysis: 主句为I think，后接宾语从句。同位语对point进行解释说明。

topics.title: Iran War and Public Opinion
topics.content: 美国与伊朗的军事冲突持续，民意调查显示战争不受欢迎，68%的受访者认为战争不值得打，而特朗普政府声称美国人并不反对战争。讨论涉及中期选举的政治影响、弹药短缺、以及油价上涨等问题。
topics.keywords: Iran war, polls, unpopular, midterms, gas prices, munitions shortage

topics.title: Nolan Wells Death Investigation
topics.content: 18岁的Nolan Wells在密西西比州岛屿附近水域失踪后死亡，独立尸检未能确定死因，仅发现头部有红色变色区域。家属和公众质疑官方调查，要求更多答案。案件涉及目击者证词、毒理学报告、以及大陪审团程序。
topics.keywords: Nolan Wells, autopsy, undetermined, independent, family, investigation, grand jury

topics.title: U.S.-Saudi Arabia Nuclear Deal
topics.content: 美国与沙特阿拉伯签署核协议，引发对中东军备竞赛的担忧。讨论涉及地区安全、石油市场、以及特朗普政府的能源政策。
topics.keywords: nuclear deal, Saudi Arabia, arms race, Middle East, oil, Strait of Hormuz

topics.title: 2026 Midterm Elections
topics.content: 距离中期选举仅100多天，战争的不受欢迎程度可能影响共和党选情。民主党利用拨款法案作为杠杆，要求对军事行动进行授权投票。
topics.keywords: midterms, GOP, Democrats, leverage, authorization, military action

topics.title: Tropical Storm Bertha
topics.content: 热带风暴Bertha预计在墨西哥湾西北海岸登陆，带来洪水和强风，影响范围从南部到中大西洋地区。
topics.keywords: Tropical Storm Bertha, landfall, flood threat, Gulf Coast, heavy rain

topics.title: ICE Shooting Incident
topics.content: 休斯顿一名男子被ICE特工枪杀，两名目击者称司机在开枪前已停车，与ICE的说法矛盾。案件仍在调查中。
topics.keywords: ICE, shooting, Houston, witness, contradiction, investigation

topics.title: Cyclosporiasis Outbreak
topics.content: FDA调查新的环孢子虫病爆发，72例病例与未确定的来源有关，此前已有数百人感染。
topics.keywords: FDA, cyclosporiasis, outbreak, investigation, food safety

topics.title: Congressional Workplace Ethics and Dating Ban
topics.content: House Speaker Mike Johnson supports a ban on lawmakers dating any House staffers, citing inappropriate workplace dynamics. The discussion highlights a culture of 'creepiness' and power imbalances on Capitol Hill, with several recent cases of sexual misconduct. The ban aims to align Congress with corporate America's standards.
topics.keywords: dating ban, workplace ethics, power dynamics, sexual misconduct, Capitol Hill culture

topics.title: U.S.-Saudi Arabia Civilian Nuclear Deal
topics.content: The Trump administration signed a 30-year deal granting Saudi Arabia a civilian nuclear program, including uranium enrichment for energy only. Critics worry about proliferation risks, while supporters argue it keeps Saudi Arabia aligned with the U.S. and counters Iranian influence. Israel expresses concern over lack of normalization progress.
topics.keywords: nuclear deal, Saudi Arabia, uranium enrichment, proliferation, normalization, Iran

topics.title: 2028 Democratic Presidential Primary Poll
topics.content: A New Hampshire poll shows Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez leading with 22%, followed by Pete Buttigieg. The discussion analyzes her appeal as a 'fighter' and her rhetorical similarities to Obama. Other potential candidates include Mark Kelly, Gavin Newsom, and Bernie Sanders. The DNC is also deciding the 2028 primary calendar order.
topics.keywords: 2028 election, Democratic primary, AOC, Pete Buttigieg, New Hampshire poll, primary calendar
